facts_disclosed: Parents Sudanese, born/raised UAE, identity tension

turns.content: hey can I ask you something kind of personal? not like advice exactly more like I need to think out loud
Of course, go ahead. Sometimes thinking out loud with someone is the best way to sort things through. What's on your mind?

so I was at this networking thing last night, architecture event downtown, and someone asked me where I'm from. and I just froze for a second. like I literally didn't know what to say. I mean I know this sounds dumb but it genuinely trips me up every single time

That doesn't sound dumb at all — it's actually a really common experience for people who have complex relationships with place and identity. What made it feel hard to answer? Is it that the truthful answer is more complicated than the question allows?

YES exactly. like ok so my parents are from Khartoum, they're Sudanese. but I was born here in Abu Dhabi. grew up here, went to school here, I've literally never lived anywhere else. so am I Sudanese?? my passport says so. but wallah I don't speak the dialect fluently, I've never been to Sudan, I can't name the streets my parents grew up on

That's a really specific kind of in-between — you carry Sudan through your family but you've never physically been there, and meanwhile the place that shaped you every day isn't reflected on your documents. What did you end up saying at the event?

I said "I'm from here" and then they were like oh Emirati? and I was like no Sudanese but born here and then you can see their brain trying to categorize you lol. it's like people need a clean label and I don't fit one. I mean, at home we eat Sudanese food and my mom plays Sudanese music and speaks Arabic with that Khartoum accent. but then I step outside and I'm in Abu Dhabi, this hyper-modern city that's also not fully mine because I'm not a citizen

You're describing something that millions of people in the Gulf experience — born and raised in a place, deeply shaped by it, but without the citizenship that would formally make it "yours." And at the same time, the homeland your family comes from is more of an idea than a lived experience for you. It's not that you don't belong anywhere — it's that you belong to something that doesn't have a simple name.

Do you feel this tension more in professional settings, or is it constant?

tbh it's worse in professional settings because architecture is SO tied to place. like I literally design buildings rooted in Gulf climate and culture and tradition. I know this landscape better than anywhere on earth. but then someone at a conference panel asks about my "home country" and suddenly I'm the outsider again. ngl it messes with my head sometimes

There's a real irony there — you're designing buildings that respond to and honor this specific place, arguably engaging with the Gulf more deeply than many people who hold the passport. Your professional identity is deeply *of* Abu Dhabi even if your legal identity doesn't fully reflect that.

Have you ever tried leaning into the complexity instead of trying to simplify it? Like: "I'm Sudanese by heritage, Emirati by upbringing, and an architect of this place by choice."

ok that's actually really beautiful?? I mean I don't think I could say that without feeling pretentious lol but the idea behind it. like owning all three things instead of apologizing for not being fully any of them. I think the problem is I keep trying to pick one answer when the real answer is that I'm all of it at once

That's exactly it. The discomfort comes from trying to fit a multi-dimensional identity into a one-word answer. And honestly, pretentious or not, your architecture *is* your claim to this place. You're not just living here — you're literally shaping the built environment. That's a deeper relationship with place than most people ever have.

Do you think your parents feel the same tension, or is it different for them since they actually lived in Sudan?

it's totally different for them. my dad still watches Sudanese news every night. my mom cooks kisra and mullah like she's keeping the whole culture alive in our kitchen. they KNOW where they're from. they chose to leave. I didn't choose anything, I was just born into this in-between space. I mean I love Abu Dhabi wallah I do. and I love my Sudanese heritage. I just wish the world had a box for people like me

Maybe the world not having that box is exactly what makes your perspective valuable — in life and in architecture. The most interesting buildings come from people who see across boundaries, who can draw from multiple traditions without being locked into one.

Your parents' Sudan is memory and choice. Yours is inheritance and imagination. Both are real, just different. And Abu Dhabi isn't less yours because of a passport — it's yours because you're building it, one villa at a time.

ok you're actually making me emotional rn lol. I think I needed to hear that. like the in-between isn't a deficit it's a perspective. wallah thank you for letting me ramble about this. I feel lighter tbh

You weren't rambling — you were working through something real and important. And the fact that you think this deeply about place and belonging probably makes you a better architect than you realize. Next time someone asks where you're from, give them whatever answer feels right in the moment. There's no wrong one. Take care of yourself.
thank you seriously. I think next time I'll just say "it's complicated" and smile lol. have a good night!!
Honestly, that might be the most powerful answer of all. Good night!
